groups.title: Start
groups.summary: Install, project shape, routing and target choices.

groups.title: Tac frontend
groups.summary: Templates, polyglot companions and browser-local data.

groups.title: Yon backend
groups.summary: Routes, middleware, validation, FYLO and observability.

groups.title: Cookbook
groups.summary: Production-shaped recipes that combine Tac, Yon and FYLO.

introduction.title: Introduction
introduction.summary: Tachyon is a polyglot, file-system-routed full-stack framework distributed through the standalone ty binary. Tac is the frontend layer, Yon is the backend/runtime layer, and FYLO is the document store beneath both.

sections.heading: Install
sections.body: Tachyon ships as the standalone `ty` binary — no package manager or runtime toolchain required. The installer also pulls the `fylo` (document store), `chex` (schema validation), and `ttid` (identifier generation) binaries it drives at runtime. Scaffold an app and start the shape-aware dev server: Tachyon detects whether your app has a client/, a server/, or both, and serves the right thing on one port.
sections.code:
curl -fsSL https://tachyon.del.ma/install.sh | sh
ty init my-app --name "My App"
cd my-app
ty serve

sections.heading: Layout
sections.body: Pages live in client/pages, components in client/components, and backend routes in server/routes. A companion file sits beside its tac.html template; shared browser assets and data live under client/shared.
sections.code:
client/
  pages/tac.html
  components/hero/tac.html
  components/hero/tac.swift
  shared/assets/  shared/data/
server/
  routes/posts/yon.js

routing.title: Routing
routing.summary: Both layers route from the file system: a directory is a path, and dynamic segments use _slug directories that become :slug parameters at runtime.

sections.heading: Frontend pages
sections.body: client/pages/tac.html serves /, client/pages/docs/tac.html serves /docs, and client/pages/docs/_topic/tac.html serves /docs/:topic — the page you are reading uses exactly that dynamic segment. An ancestor page containing a slot element becomes a reusable shell for its descendants.
sections.code:
client/pages/
  tac.html          -> /
  docs/tac.html     -> /docs (shell with <slot />)
  docs/_topic/
    tac.html        -> /docs/:topic

sections.heading: Backend routes
sections.body: A route file at server/routes/<path>/yon.<ext> exports a Handler class whose static methods are HTTP verbs. One route can be split across languages with sibling yon files declaring non-overlapping verbs — the Tachyon repo's full-stack fixture app splits its /language route across JavaScript (GET/HEAD), Rust (POST/DELETE) and C++ (PUT/PATCH).

sections.heading: Validation
sections.body: An OPTIONS.schema.json beside each handler validates requests and responses with CHEX regex schemas, and feeds the generated OpenAPI 3.1 document served at /openapi.json with a docs UI at /api-docs.

yon.title: Yon backend
yon.summary: Yon is Tachyon's backend/runtime layer: filesystem-routed handlers, polyglot process adapters, CHEX validation, OpenAPI docs, FYLO data routes, SSE realtime, middleware, rate limiting and OpenTelemetry.

sections.heading: Serve modes
sections.body: ty serve is shape-aware. A client/ app serves frontend assets, a server/ app serves backend routes, a client/ + server/ app runs full-stack, and client/ + db/ can mount Tachyon's built-in FYLO browser routes when YON_DATA_BROWSER_ENABLED=true.
sections.code:
ty serve
YON_PORT=8000 ty serve
YON_HOST=0.0.0.0 ty serve
NODE_ENV=production ty serve

sections.heading: Route convention
sections.body: Backend routes live at server/routes/<path>/yon.<ext>. A Handler class exposes static methods named after HTTP verbs. Dynamic path segments use _name folders and arrive in request.paths.

sections.heading: Languages
sections.body: Yon ships ergonomic adapters for JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, Ruby, PHP, Dart, Java, C#, C++ and Rust. The adapter path lets authors write only Handler methods. Any other executable language can still run by speaking Yon JSON over stdin/stdout and declaring methods in OPTIONS.schema.json.
sections.code:
server/yon.providers.js     # optional app-defined providers
.tachyonrc                 # optional interpreter overrides

sections.heading: Process lifecycle
sections.body: Yon invokes route handlers as processes and prewarms handlers after route discovery. When NODE_ENV=production, compiled-language adapters cache their build artifacts so later requests avoid recompilation work.
sections.code:
YON_HANDLER_TIMEOUT_MS=30000
YON_MAX_BODY_BYTES=1048576

sections.heading: Validation and status selection
sections.body: OPTIONS.schema.json is strictly CHEX. Request schemas validate declared request sections, response schemas validate the returned body, and status-code schemas let Yon infer the final HTTP status from the body shape.

sections.heading: OpenAPI and docs UI
sections.body: Yon generates OpenAPI 3.1 from registered routes and OPTIONS.schema.json files. The JSON document is served at /openapi.json and the self-hosted Tachyon API docs UI is served at /api-docs by default.
sections.code:
curl http://localhost:8080/openapi.json
open http://localhost:8080/api-docs

sections.heading: FYLO routes
sections.body: When YON_DATA_BROWSER_ENABLED=true, Yon mounts /_fylo for collection inspection and REST-style document operations. Keep it readonly by default; enable mutation only behind auth, explicit origins and rate limiting.
sections.code:
YON_DATA_BROWSER_ENABLED=true
YON_DATA_BROWSER_READONLY=true
YON_DATA_BROWSER_READONLY=false

sections.heading: Realtime mailboxes
sections.body: YON_REALTIME_ENABLED mounts /_yon/realtime. Clients register a TTID, hold or reopen an SSE stream, and messages are stored durably in FYLO-backed topic logs for replay after reconnect.
sections.code:
YON_REALTIME_ENABLED=true
curl -X POST /_yon/realtime/clients
curl '/_yon/realtime/stream?clientId=<TTID>'

sections.heading: Security and middleware
sections.body: Yon rejects disallowed origins before route execution, applies security headers, supports Basic Auth hashes, request/body limits, middleware before/after hooks, and an optional app-provided rateLimiter.take(). Legacy middleware.js still works, and polyglot middleware can live at server/middleware/yon.<ext> or middleware/yon.<ext>.
sections.code:
YON_ALLOW_ORIGINS=https://app.example
YON_BASIC_AUTH_HASH=...
YON_RATE_LIMIT_MAX=120
YON_RATE_LIMIT_WINDOW_MS=60000

sections.heading: Polyglot middleware
sections.body: Use middleware.js when you want direct JavaScript Request and Response objects. Use polyglot middleware when the app team wants the same cross-language process model as Yon routes. Tachyon looks for server/middleware/yon.<ext> first, then middleware/yon.<ext>.
sections.code:
server/
  middleware/
    yon.py          # class-style Middleware adapter
  routes/
    items/yon.ts

# or, for package-level middleware:
middleware/
  yon.rb

sections.heading: Class-style middleware
sections.body: JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, Ruby and PHP can define a Middleware class. before can return a response envelope to stop the request, after can add headers or replace the response, and rateLimit can return the same decision object as middleware.js rateLimiter.take().

sections.heading: Raw shim middleware
sections.body: Any other language can skip the class adapter and use the raw protocol. The middleware file reads a JSON envelope from stdin and writes one JSON result to stdout. This is useful for Go, Swift, Zig, shell scripts, or a prebuilt binary.

sections.heading: Middleware envelopes
sections.body: Process middleware receives metadata-only envelopes by default so it cannot accidentally consume request bodies or break streaming responses. The before envelope includes phase, request and context. The after envelope also includes response metadata. Result envelopes can continue, respond early, replace a response, or return a rate-limit decision.

sections.heading: Telemetry
sections.body: With YON_OTEL_ENABLED=true, Yon creates request and handler spans, continues traceparent, returns Traceparent and X-Trace-Id headers, and persists OTLP JSON trace documents into a FYLO collection.
sections.code:
YON_OTEL_ENABLED=true
YON_OTEL_SERVICE_NAME=my-api
YON_OTEL_COLLECTION=otel-spans

templates.title: Tac templates
templates.summary: Templates are HTML with braces for interpolation, on:event attributes for delegated events, loop, logic, and switch elements for control flow, and explicit island hydration policies.

sections.heading: Interpolation and events
sections.body: Braces evaluate JavaScript inside the async render function, so await is available anywhere. Event handlers are single expressions compiled to one delegated listener per event type, registered at the document and hydrated off the critical path with lossless replay.
sections.code: {headline}

sections.heading: Control flow
sections.body: Loops, conditionals and switches are elements, so structure stays visible in the markup.
sections.code:
{post.title}

Ready.

Working…

Done.

sections.heading: Components
sections.body: A component folder under client/components maps to a hyphenated tag: components/panel/users becomes panel-users. Props pass as attributes, and adding the lazy attribute defers loading until the component scrolls into view.

sections.heading: Island hydration
sections.body: A component occurrence with a literal hydrate policy is fully rendered on the server, then adopts that useful DOM in place when its browser companion activates. Use load for immediate activation, idle for background work, visible for viewport-driven activation, interaction for the first user intent, or never for permanently static server HTML. Repeated islands, including components inside loop, receive independent identities.
sections.code:
<product-card :product="product" hydrate="visible" />
<cart-summary hydrate="interaction" />
<legal-notice hydrate="never" />

sections.heading: Island contracts
sections.body: hydrate must be one of load, idle, visible, interaction, or never as a static literal. It cannot be dynamic or combined with lazy. Props must be JSON-serializable public data; never put secrets or authorization state in serialized island props. A failed activation leaves the server-rendered DOM in place and can retry on a later interaction.

native-rendering.title: Native HTML rendering
native-rendering.summary: Non-web Tac bundles keep HTML as the only application-facing view language, render supported semantics with native controls, and isolate only unmapped subtrees in local WebViews.

sections.heading: Native by default
sections.body: web remains a browser target. macos, windows, linux, ios, and android are always native-first. The compiler emits tachyon.native-ui.json for the initial versioned UI tree and tachyon.native-controller.js for live rendering and events; neither controller nor native nodes depend on a DOM.
sections.code:
ty bundle --target macos
ty bundle --target android
ty bundle --target windows

sections.heading: One HTML source
sections.body: Application developers still write tac.html. Before native tree creation, Tac lowers loop, logic, switch and case control elements, resolves Tac components, evaluates bindings, and removes nonvisual script, style, template, and noscript elements. Native hosts map the resulting semantic tree to SwiftUI on Apple platforms, Jetpack Compose on Android, WinUI on Windows, and GTK on Linux.
sections.code:
{title}

Selected: {selected}

sections.heading: Native-first tag handling
sections.body: Control tags and Tac component tags compile away before native output. Supported semantic HTML maps to native controls. Known standard HTML without a native mapping and unmapped Web Components automatically become isolated WebView boundaries; a genuinely unknown non-HTML tag still fails so typos remain visible. A semantic nativeUIAdapters mapping always wins over fallback.

sections.heading: Live events
sections.body: Each native host sends normalized input and event data to the generated controller, including elementId, type, value, checked, and detail. The controller applies the event, rerenders, and returns the complete next tree for the host to reconcile. JavaScriptCore runs the controller on macOS and iOS; Android, Windows, and Linux use embedded QuickJS runtimes.

sections.heading: Automatic local boundary
sections.body: The compiler keeps supported parents and siblings native while loading the nearest unmapped subtree in an isolated local WebView with bundled CSS and module entrypoints. No app-wide mode or fallback declaration is required. Truly unknown non-HTML tags still fail, and local boundaries do not grant raw nativeCapabilities, devicePermissions, managed content origins, or native host extensions.

companions.title: Companions
companions.summary: A tac.js or tac.ts file beside a template is instantiated automatically; its fields and methods are visible to the template without wiring.

sections.heading: Reactive fields
sections.body: Assigning to a declared field schedules one batched rerender of the component subtree. Fields prefixed with $ persist to sessionStorage; $$ fields persist to localStorage. Props auto-bind onto same-named declared fields.

sections.heading: Decorators
sections.body: Stage 3 decorators expose the same helpers declaratively — publish and subscribe for cross-component signals, env for allowlisted browser config, and onMount for lifecycle. The compiler auto-imports them.

polyglot.title: Polyglot companions
polyglot.summary: Tac companions are colocated controllers for templates. They are portable across web, desktop and mobile outputs and do not require a client worker endpoint.

sections.heading: Choose a companion language
sections.body: A component or page may use tac.js, tac.ts, tac.dart, tac.rs, tac.kt, tac.swift, or tac.cs beside its tac.html file. JavaScript and TypeScript use Bun, Dart uses the managed Dart bridge, and Rust, Kotlin, Swift, and C# use Tachyon's portable companion subset.

sections.heading: Use native-shaped shims
sections.body: Companion source never calls Tac transport APIs. Every language gets an implicit platform prelude for local/session storage, browser context, local-first FYLO collections and capability-checked device calls. Browser output services standards-backed operations today; unavailable and native-only operations remain feature-detectable and fail closed. Raw OPFS handles remain private to FYLO's bundled browser engine.

sections.heading: Contracts stay on the server
sections.body: OPTIONS.schema.json remains the Yon route contract for incoming requests and outgoing responses. Tac companions are UI controllers, so they call normal HTTP endpoints through the local-first fetch binding rather than declaring a second route protocol.

sections.heading: Device boundary
sections.body: Browser builds use standards-backed implementations or fail closed when a capability is unavailable. Native-first hosts do not yet service the device prelude: devicePermissions and nativeCapabilities are rejected until each family has an audited native-tree adapter. Keep capability checks around target-specific calls so the same source remains honest as adapters land.

devices.title: Devices and targets
devices.summary: Tac builds one client source tree into a browser output or native desktop and mobile controls. The rooted terminology: platform is the form factor (desktop/mobile/web); environment and os are synonyms for the concrete host (windows/macos/linux/android/ios/web); target is the bundle output.

sections.heading: Targets
sections.body: The supported bundle targets are web, macos, windows, linux, ios and android. web emits browser assets; every non-web target uses the native renderer by default. The all target expands to every supported target and writes sibling directories under dist/.
sections.code:
ty bundle --target web      # dist/web
ty bundle --target macos    # dist/macos
ty bundle --target windows  # dist/windows
ty bundle --target linux    # dist/linux
ty bundle --target ios      # dist/ios
ty bundle --target android  # dist/android
ty bundle --target all

sections.heading: One native-first policy
sections.body: Every non-web target maps supported Tac HTML to platform controls and automatically isolates the nearest unmapped standard HTML or Web Component subtree in a local WebView. Native siblings remain native. The former render-mode flag and TAC_RENDER_MODE override have been removed.
sections.code:
ty bundle --target macos    # SwiftUI + inferred local boundaries
ty bundle --target android  # Compose + inferred local boundaries
ty bundle --target windows  # WinUI + inferred local boundaries

sections.heading: Preview
sections.body: Preview accepts exactly one target. If you omit --target, Tachyon previews web. Native previews run prerequisite checks first so missing Xcode or Swift, the Android SDK, Windows build tools, GTK, embedded controller dependencies, or a WebView dependency required by an inferred boundary fail early with an actionable message.
sections.code:
ty preview --target web
ty preview --target macos
ty preview --target android

sections.heading: Runtime globals
sections.body: Templates can read platform, environment, os and target without imports. Every companion language can read the same structured context through its Tac runtime binding.
sections.code:
<switch :value="platform">
  <case :when="'desktop'">Desktop bundle on {os}</case>
  <case :when="'mobile'">Mobile bundle on {os}</case>
  <case default>Web platform</case>
</switch>

const platform = this.tac.platform.platform  // desktop | mobile | web
const os = this.tac.platform.os              // windows | macos | linux | android | ios | web

sections.heading: Rooted terminology
sections.body: platform is the form factor: macos, windows and linux targets are the desktop platform; ios and android are the mobile platform; web is the web platform. environment and os are synonyms for the concrete host: the operating system for native bundles, web for browser bundles (the detected visitor OS stays on browserOS). target names the bundle output. Companion language names the language a Tac companion is written in: JavaScript, TypeScript, Dart, Rust, Kotlin, Swift or C#.

storage.title: Storage and data
storage.summary: FYLO is filesystem-first: collections, indexes, event journals and WORM history all live under one root that Yon serves through /_fylo.

sections.heading: Local-first browser reads
sections.body: Inside Tac scripts, fetch() caches successful GET responses into IndexedDB. Every companion language can use the bundled FYLO browser engine through its implicit fylo collection facade; it mirrors documents into OPFS with cache-first, network-first, reload and no-store policies.

sections.heading: Live subscriptions
sections.body: subscribe() runs an initial find, listens to the browser-local mirror for same-tab mutations, and follows the server event stream for remote changes — falling back to polling when SSE is unavailable.

sections.heading: Realtime mailboxes
sections.body: Yon realtime persists messages into durable FYLO queues and delivers them over Server-Sent Events; reconnecting clients replay from their last cursor, so offline browsers never lose messages.

cookbook.title: Cookbook
cookbook.summary: Concrete recipes for building Tachyon apps without leaving the frontend-first workflow. This website is frontend-only, so these patterns can be explored with Tac companions and the browser FYLO client before a Yon server exists.

sections.heading: Frontend-only prototype
sections.body: Start with client/ and db/ only. Tac renders pages and components, companions provide the reactive UI controller, and the browser FYLO client stores documents locally. When you later add server/routes, the UI contract can stay the same.
sections.code:
client/
  pages/tac.html
  components/panel/users/tac.html
  components/panel/users/tac.kt
  shared/data/docs.json
db/
  schemas/users/manifest.json

sections.heading: Local-first collection
sections.body: JavaScript and TypeScript can use fylo.users or fylo.collection('users'). Dart, Kotlin, Swift and C# use fylo.collection('users'); Rust uses fylo().collection('users'). Tachyon lowers each form to the same browser mirror. The engine keeps OPFS private and can synchronize with Yon /_fylo routes when a backend is present.

sections.heading: Signal-driven UI
sections.body: Publish and subscribe decorators replace manual event wiring between companion scripts. A form can publish a collection change and every interested panel can refresh itself without knowing who sent the signal.

sections.heading: Polyglot middleware boundary
sections.body: When the frontend prototype grows a Yon server, add middleware at server/middleware/yon.<ext>. Class-style middleware works for the supported adapter languages, and the raw JSON shim lets a team keep an existing policy engine in another executable language.

sections.heading: Native target polish
sections.body: Design once against the environment and platform globals, then bundle to web, desktop and mobile outputs. The same Tac template can choose browser, desktop or mobile copy without creating separate pages.
sections.code:
<switch :value="environment">
  <case :when="'desktop'">Desktop workspace for {platform}</case>
  <case :when="'mobile'">Pocket workspace for {platform}</case>
  <case default>Browser workspace</case>
</switch>

operations.title: Operations
operations.summary: Health and readiness endpoints, generated OpenAPI docs, middleware hooks, rate limiting, and OpenTelemetry spans persisted into FYLO come built in.

sections.heading: Observability
sections.body: With YON_OTEL_ENABLED, Yon writes request and handler spans as OTLP JSON into the otel-spans collection, continues incoming traceparent headers, and emits Traceparent and X-Trace-Id on responses. This site's telemetry panel reads them back from /language/telemetry.

sections.heading: Build and deploy
sections.body: ty bundle writes dist/web by default. Use --target all during release validation, then preview or package the individual target you intend to ship.
sections.code:
ty bundle --target all
ty preview --target web
ty preview --target macos

sections.heading: Security
sections.body: Security headers on every response, hashed Basic Auth, request limits and handler timeouts, origin-aware CORS rejection, and schema validation are all on by default in production.

platform-apis.title: Platform APIs
platform-apis.summary: The web target exposes standards-backed capability helpers today. Native-first targets fail closed until each privileged family has a concrete native-tree adapter.

sections.heading: Current target availability
sections.body: Browser output can use storage, fetch, clipboard, file selection, geolocation, notifications, and media when the browser supports them. capabilities.supports reports whether an operation is available, and capabilities.state reports granted, denied, prompt, or unsupported where a permission state exists.

sections.heading: Native-first fail-closed boundary
sections.body: macos, windows, linux, ios, and android now use native controls plus a DOM-free controller. Native-tree capability adapters are not implemented yet, so bundles reject devicePermissions, nativeCapabilities, managedContentOrigins, and nativeHostExtensions. An automatic local WebView boundary renders only its unsupported subtree and never receives an application-wide native bridge.

sections.heading: Portable companion spelling
sections.body: Companion source stays transport-free. JavaScript and TypeScript use camelCase helpers; Dart, Kotlin, and Swift use the same family names; Rust and C# keep language-native casing. Unavailable operations such as auth.verifyUser and filesystem access remain detectable by name and fail closed instead of silently falling back.

sections.heading: Events and future adapters
sections.body: host.on is the typed event channel for capability implementations that provide host events. A native-tree adapter must define its operation contract, target implementation, permission model, failure behavior, and tests before Tachyon enables the family on non-web targets. Camera, biometrics, managed content, filesystem access, and extensions remain explicit roadmap work rather than compatibility promises.
